steps.title: Identify what the conclusion requires
steps.content: The team concluded that the ability to distinguish spontaneous from volitional laughter is universal across cultures. The correct finding must show (1) participants could distinguish the laugh types above chance, and (2) this held equally across societies.

steps.title: Evaluate each choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — An assessment of the effect of demographic variables on judgments of laughter type in Austria, Samoa, and the other societies in the study revealed that participants' evaluations were correlated with the economic conditions of the societies in which participants live.: Ties recognition to economic variables — a factor that varies by culture — which would undermine a universal claim. ✗
  • Choice B — The overall rate of correct judgments by participants was 64%, substantially better than chance, and spontaneous and volitional laughs were recognized at similar rates in Austria, Samoa, and the other societies in the study.: Shows above-chance accuracy (supporting the ability to distinguish) and similar rates across societies (supporting universality). ✓
  • Choice C — Acoustic features of spontaneous laughter, such as pitch and intensity, have measurable variations both within and across societies.: Describes properties of the laughs themselves, not whether listeners across cultures can correctly identify them. ✗
  • Choice D — A variety of people from Austria and Samoa evaluated the recordings in the study, and no significant differences in listeners' judgments of laughter types were found.: Limited to just two societies; does not speak to the other 19 societies needed to claim a universal finding. ✗
That is the correct answer.
